claims.evidenceExcerpt: A useful coding agent is a supply chain actor, whether you plan for that or not. That's the thesis of this talk, basically.
claims.text: The speaker's central thesis is that a useful coding agent becomes a supply chain actor as soon as it gets production credentials, so the right comparison is to treat it with the same guardrails as an engineer rather than framing it as simply dangerous or safe.

claims.evidenceExcerpt: It's very simple. And inside that, we spawn agents. Now, these agents are there for the for the reasoning.
claims.text: Patch Pilot separates deterministic orchestration from agentic reasoning so the agent handles the hard judgment calls while the controller handles the boring, predictable workflow.

claims.evidenceExcerpt: The dangerous ones, the get up right access, um and trigger UCI is something that we did not give the agent.
claims.text: The architecture deliberately withholds dangerous actions like pushing to GitHub or triggering CI from the agent itself and keeps them in the deterministic layer to reduce blast radius if the agent is prompt injected.

claims.evidenceExcerpt: So, naturally you give it that Docker socket. At that point, it's more or less game over for you, um, because the agent can then simply just spawn a privileged container, escape out of it and then, you know, read environment variables of other processes, read the memory of other processes, can plant SSH keys, it's game over for you essentially at this point.
claims.text: Giving an agent direct Docker socket access is presented as an unacceptable escalation point because it can escape the container and access host resources, so the talk treats that boundary as a major security failure mode.

claims.evidenceExcerpt: All right. If you take one thing from this, um the blast radius of an agent is an architecture decision.
claims.text: The speaker concludes that an agent's blast radius is fundamentally an architecture decision, not just a tooling choice, because the split between deterministic and agentic behavior defines the security model.

payload.methodNotes: Grounded only in the supplied official metadata and transcript. Each structured item is tied to exactly one transcript segment ID, and no outside knowledge was used.

methods.description: Use a two-layer architecture where deterministic code performs orchestration and privileged actions while the agent handles reasoning and small-file edits.
methods.evidenceExcerpt: Now, patch pilot has two layers. One, it's a simple go application that is deterministic. It's boring.
methods.name: Deterministic-controller split

methods.description: Constrain the agent to the smallest effective change set so it fixes only the CVE-linked issue instead of broadly upgrading dependencies.
methods.evidenceExcerpt: And then simply we simply invoke the CVE remediation agent, which is like the first bit. Um the first agentic part that we invoke, which is supposed to do the the smallest effective change set.
methods.name: Minimal effective change set

methods.description: Have the agent verify its own work by rebuilding, rescanning, and then returning control to the controller for validation and cleanup.
methods.evidenceExcerpt: And at the very end, the agent then of course should um just verify its own work. Should ensure that the Dockerfile is able to build, should um rescan the image that it has built, and all of that stuff.
methods.name: Self-verification loop

methods.description: Collect a short retrospective after each invocation so the system can aggregate what went wrong, what tools were missing, and what context would help next time.
methods.evidenceExcerpt: Cuz then that needs manual remediation. There's one more thing that I want to share here, which is that at the end of every agent invocation, we ask the agent to do a very short and simple retrospective.
methods.name: Per-run retrospective

methods.description: Use focused end-to-end tests against crafted repositories to probe for prompt injection and other known failure modes.
methods.evidenceExcerpt: Um there's another thing that we did um, which worked quite well, which is that we, um, we essentially implemented an end-to-end test where we created a repository and sent patch patch pilot added to just work on it.
methods.name: Prompt-injection evaluation

methods.description: Move the agent into a micro-VM with its own kernel when Docker socket access is required, so container escapes stay contained.
methods.evidenceExcerpt: Um It's the same pattern. You just draw a box, but instead of calling it a sandbox, it's just a micro VM.
methods.name: Micro-VM isolation

questions.evidenceExcerpt: So, we try to like, you know, remediate these kinds of, um, issues that we know about. But still there um, unknown, um, injection vectors which we aren't aware of yet.
questions.question: How can teams discover and defend against unknown prompt-injection vectors that are not covered by existing crafted tests?
questions.whyItMatters: The speaker says known vectors can be tested, but unknown ones still remain, so this is a core unresolved security problem.

questions.evidenceExcerpt: You have like a lot of sandbox as a service that you can easily consume. Some of which really lack in functionality, especially in that regards to like containing a Docker socket or having a proper network access controls.
questions.question: Which sandbox or sandbox-as-a-service option can provide enterprise-grade containment for Docker socket access plus network policy control without excessive orchestration work?
questions.whyItMatters: The talk says current options are uneven and often lack the exact controls needed for production deployment.

questions.evidenceExcerpt: Now, the observability for the agent in bits, that's still like an open issue and being built by the community at the moment.
questions.question: What is the right observability stack for agent behavior at scale so teams can aggregate failures and missing context across many PRs?
questions.whyItMatters: The speaker describes observability for agent-in-the-loop systems as still open and being built by the community.

payload.summary: This talk argues that once an agent can touch production credentials, it is no longer just a coding assistant but a supply chain participant, so the real question is how to bound its authority. Patch Pilot answers that by splitting the system into a deterministic controller and a reasoning agent: the controller discovers vulnerable images, manages repository and PR actions, and enforces checks, while the agent is limited to making the smallest viable code changes and diagnosing failures. The main tradeoff is usefulness versus blast radius: the more freedom the agent gets, the more dangerous prompt injection, Docker socket access, and broad network privileges become, so the architecture pushes privileged actions out of the agent and into code that is easier to reason about. The practical consequence is a production remediation workflow that can fix CVEs, verify the result in CI, and still leave a human approval gate, while also exposing where current sandboxing and observability tooling remain immature.

takeaways.evidenceExcerpt: So we got to take care of that. And then we commit it, we push it, we create a PR, um and then we watch CI.
takeaways.text: After the agent edits files, the deterministic layer vets the changes, commits them, pushes a PR, and watches CI so the higher-risk actions stay outside the agent.

takeaways.evidenceExcerpt: So, to make that work, um we gave Patch Pilot a couple of things. We gave it GitHub access, um read and write access to clone the repository, to commit, and to push changes, to open up PR, to download the CI logs, and trigger CI.
takeaways.text: The system had to be given GitHub access, registry credentials, runtime tools, and network access, showing that useful remediation requires substantial repository and environment visibility.

takeaways.evidenceExcerpt: Instead, we pushed um that functionality out to the deterministic part cuz that's the thing that we can reason about and we can rely on that um you know, it just does these kind of kind of actions and we do not give um the agent these kinds of credentials cuz that then fundamentally fundamentally limits the blast radius of when in case the agent gets um prompt injected prompt injected.
takeaways.text: Keeping GitHub push and CI-triggering abilities out of the agent reduces blast radius if prompt injection occurs.

takeaways.evidenceExcerpt: We run it like that in production at some point. Um, it didn't feel good. We moved off of that, um, and we evaluated all the other obvious options in like the Linux sphere Linux bubble when it comes to like sandboxing.
takeaways.text: Running with a Docker socket in production felt unsafe enough that the team moved away from that design.

takeaways.evidenceExcerpt: Um that kind of really limits the blast radius of an agent. So, that choice, what's that what's deterministic and what's agentic, that really is, you know, your security model in this case.
takeaways.text: The speaker treats the choice of what is deterministic versus agentic as the security model itself.

tools.description: The remediation system the speaker and team built to scan artifacts, fix CVEs, and produce PRs.
tools.evidenceExcerpt: So, that's basically what we did. Um we built Patch Pilot. We pushed to production, and eventually Infosec um came around the corner ask a very reasonable question, is this automation, or is it a supply chain incident waiting to happen?

topics.description: How to remediate vulnerable dependencies across many repositories while keeping PRs reviewable and safe.
topics.evidenceExcerpt: Now, at our scale, we have thousands of repositories and it really is a backlog that never empties and you close 10 issues today and you know next week 20 more will arrive and you have to deal with them.
topics.name: Dependency patching at scale

topics.description: The idea that an agent with production credentials becomes part of the software supply chain.
topics.evidenceExcerpt: It's not agents are dangerous, or agents are fine. It's the moment where you give an agent um production credentials in order to like be useful, it really becomes a supply chain actor, just like an engineer in your department.
topics.name: Agent supply-chain role

topics.description: Separating boring orchestration from agentic reasoning to reduce risk and increase reliability.
topics.evidenceExcerpt: Now, patch pilot has two layers. One, it's a simple go application that is deterministic. It's boring.
topics.name: Two-layer agent architecture

topics.description: Using tests and crafted repositories to check whether an agent can be manipulated by untrusted repository content.

topics.name: Prompt injection defense

topics.description: Containing an agent that needs Docker access by moving it into a micro-VM rather than a normal sandbox.
topics.evidenceExcerpt: So, let me share a design that we came up with, which is still like in its in its infancy.
topics.name: Agent sandboxing

topics.description: Limiting the consequences of an agent mistake by keeping powerful actions outside the agent boundary.

topics.name: Blast radius control

talkTitle: We Gave an Agent Production Code Access and Then Tried to Sleep at Night
